title: The turn to the imagination: transformation, politics and limits [Critical Exchange]
author: Machin, Sachs Olsen, Death, Diehl, Hatzisavvidou, Marchart, Malzacher

opening_move.description: The exchange opens by naming and mapping a cross-disciplinary 'turn to the imagination': the concept of imaginaries is proliferating across the academy and beyond as a tool for understanding how political transformation is provoked, harnessed, and inhibited. The exchange asks what this turn means for political theory.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Understanding the political imagination — its creative and destructive potential — is essential for theorising democratic transformation in the present moment of far-right resurgence and ecological crisis

theoretical_positioning.description: A 7-author exchange mapping the political theory of imagination across multiple domains: far-right imaginaries, anti-colonial and decolonial imaginaries, postgrowth/ecosocialist/ecofeminist imaginaries, and theatre as political means. Draws on Castoriadis, Lefort, Laclau, Fanon, Mbembe, Yusoff.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: conceptual analysis of social imaginaries; close reading of political theorists; analysis of theatrical practice as political form
epistemic_mode.notes: Critical exchange (28 pages, 7 authors). Treated as single profile. The exchange uses theatre and political movements as interpretive cases to develop a normative account of political imagination and its transformative potential. No empirical data; argument is normative-theoretical with interpretive cases.

case_type.case_name: Far-right imaginaries; anti-colonial movements; ecosocialist/ecofeminist practice; theatre as political form

confidence_notes: Critical exchange (28 pages, 7 authors). Treated as single profile. The exchange's breadth across multiple imaginaries and political domains makes clean typology assignment moderately difficult; 'normative_with_interpretive_case' best captures the use of political movements and theatre as generative interpretive objects for a normative theory of political imagination.
